ground_truth.title: How to Plan an Event: a Complete Guide
ground_truth.author: WildApricot
ground_truth.main_content: Looking for advice on how to plan an event? You've come to the right place! We've seen a lot of organizations' events over the years. And while we can't identify every last detail you'll need to think about (it often depends on the type of event!) we've got a bushel of best practices that work well for everybody. Whether you're new to running an event or are just looking for some refreshers, this post will cover information including: Why you need an event plan, The core features of an event management plan, A free event planning template (plus checklists for in person and virtual events!), How to plan a successful event in 13 steps. An event plan is a guide for all the components of your event. Also called an event management plan, this tool organizes all of the steps and key information that go into running an event. Whether you're neck-deep in conference planning or setting up your first small park event, an event plan serves as your North Star as you prepare for the big day. A big part of knowing how to run an event successfully is keeping all of the components in one place—which is where an event plan comes in! With a clear event plan example, all you need to do is fill in the blanks. Your event management plan should cover: Event goals and objectives, Individual roles and responsibilities, Volunteers and volunteer training, Budget, Date and time of the event, Event master plan, Event location, Event management software, Event Branding, Event speakers and special guests, Partnerships and event sponsorship opportunities, Promotional, advertising, marketing, and publicity plan, Day-of processes, Vendor information. Planning a successful event comes down to following these 13 steps: Set your goals, Organize your team, Recruit and train volunteers, Establish (and stick to!) your budget, Choose a date, Create an event master plan, Choose your event software, Book your venue, Brand your event, Confirm your special guests, Connect with partners and sponsors, Execute a strong event marketing plan, Set up your day-of processes. The very first step in planning a successful event is to establish tangible goals and objectives. Ask yourself these questions: Why am I organizing this event? What do I hope to achieve? Who is this event for? What are my metrics of success? Any event takes a concerted team effort to handle all the details! A team of dedicated staff members can execute the task lists, and your board of directors can use their networks and knowledge to help with the big picture. Some event planning roles to delegate include: Event planning team, Volunteer coordinator, Publicity and communications strategist, Venue management specialist, Vendor coordinator, Partner and sponsor liaison, Fundraising and donor management, Marketing and branding experts. Rockstar volunteers are key to planning a successful event. But before you begin working with them, make sure you understand what their roles are and how you can begin recruiting them. Establishing your event's budget is one of the most important parts of planning an event. Creating a solid budget enables you and your team to generate ideas within realistic parameters. Some of the critical expenses you need to include in your budget for running an event are: Venue, Food and drink, Entertainment, Décor, Staff, Marketing, Software, Hybrid and virtual conference costs, A/V, Miscellaneous. The date might already be pre-set for a recurring event, but if you're planning an event that's new, there are some things to keep in mind. Be sure to consider the following before firming up your date: Give yourself enough time (ideally 4-6 months), Be aware of statutory and religious holidays, Avoid school holiday time periods, Check dates with key participants. Once you know all the costs and the timeline associated with your event, it's time to start the real plan! Your event master plan should encompass all aspects of the event, including: Venue, logistics & catering management, Speakers and presenters, Creating a conference agenda and schedule, Activities and entertainment, Publicity and promotion, Registration, Sponsor and partner management, Volunteer management and responsibilities. The right event software can make all the difference in streamlining the processes in your event master plan. Types of event software that can be worthwhile having include: Registration, Ticketing, Event website, Mobile app. Once you have a venue and date locked in, it's time to book the rest of your vendors. This includes: Catering, A/V and production, Entertainment, Transportation and parking, Security, Photography and videography, Signage and wayfinding. Event branding is what sets the tone for your event and what helps attendees remember it. Event branding encompasses everything from your event name and logo to your color scheme, typography, imagery, and messaging. A cohesive brand will make your event look professional and put together, and it will also help with marketing. To book speakers, you'll want to: Start with your network, Research relevant experts, Consider your audience, Reach out with a clear value proposition, Negotiate terms, Build relationships. Sponsorships are a great way to offset costs and increase the credibility of your event. To find partners and sponsors: Identify potential sponsors, Create a sponsorship package, Reach out, Deliver on your promises. A strong event marketing plan is essential to the success of your event. To market your event: Define your target audience, Create a marketing timeline, Use multiple channels, Leverage your existing audience, Track your results. To set up your day-of processes: Create a detailed run of show, Prepare for contingencies, Brief your staff and volunteers, Test everything, Have a communication plan. If you're looking for a bit more structure for planning an event, we've created a Free Event Planning Checklist you can simply download and modify to your organization's needs.
